Course: Environmental Policy and Economics

Professor: The fast-fashion industry produces enormous textile waste and pollution. Do you think governments are justified in restricting cheap, disposable clothing, or should consumers remain free to decide for themselves?

Farah: I think restrictions are justified. Fast fashion's true costs, polluted rivers and mountains of waste, are hidden from the price tag and paid by everyone else. When a product's real damage is invisible to the buyer, leaving it purely to consumer choice can't fix the problem.
Lukas: I'm wary of governments dictating what people wear. Cheap clothing genuinely helps lower-income families, and heavy restrictions could price them out. I'd rather see honest labeling and taxes on pollution so companies clean up, while shoppers keep the freedom to choose.
similar.model_essay: I come down closer to Farah, though Lukas points to how restrictions should be shaped. The core problem is that fast fashion's real costs, contaminated water and overflowing landfills, never appear on the price tag, so the cheapness is partly an illusion paid for by others. That market failure is exactly why pure consumer choice can't solve it, as Farah argues. Still, Lukas is right that outright bans would hurt families who rely on affordable clothing. The better tool sits between them: make producers pay for the waste and pollution they create, through fees that fund recycling and honest environmental labeling. That raises prices on the most wasteful items while preserving affordable options, and it targets the industry's behavior rather than punishing the shopper for a system built around disposability.

Course: Environmental Policy and Economics

Professor: Some governments have set firm deadlines to ban the sale of new gasoline cars, while others prefer to let incentives and market forces phase them out gradually. Which approach do you think is more justified?

Mira: I support firm deadlines. Automakers and buyers only make big changes when the endpoint is certain; a fixed cutoff gives everyone a clear target to plan around. Left to drift, the transition stalls because there's always a reason to delay one more year.
Hendrik: I prefer letting incentives lead. A hard ban risks stranding people who can't yet afford or charge an electric car, especially in rural areas. Rebates and better charging networks pull the market forward without forcing anyone off the road before the alternatives are truly ready.
similar.model_essay: I side with Mira's firm deadline, while treating Hendrik's concerns as design conditions rather than reasons to abandon it. Mira's central insight is that certainty drives investment: once automakers know gasoline sales end on a fixed date, they redirect research and factories accordingly, and buyers plan ahead. Voluntary incentives, by contrast, always leave room to postpone another year. Hendrik is right that a poorly timed ban could strand rural or lower-income drivers, but the deadline usually applies to new-car sales, not existing vehicles, and is set a decade out. That gap is exactly when Hendrik's rebates and charging networks should be built, so alternatives are ready when the cutoff arrives. A deadline sets the destination; incentives ease the journey.

Professor: To cut container waste, some regions require a refundable deposit on every bottle and can, while others rely on voluntary curbside recycling. Do you think mandatory deposit schemes are justified, or should recycling remain voluntary?

Sanne: I strongly support deposit schemes. Attaching real money to each container transforms behavior overnight, and the regions that use them recover the vast majority of their bottles. Voluntary recycling, however well-intentioned, consistently leaves most containers in the trash or scattered as litter.
Oscar: I see the appeal, but deposits add cost and hassle, and they single out one kind of waste. I'd rather improve curbside recycling for everything at once. A well-run collection system can lift recycling broadly without making people haul bottles back to a machine.

Better curbside recycling, which I would also support, complements deposits rather than replacing them. Given how reliably deposits outperform good intentions, the minor inconvenience seems a small price to pay.

professor: Imagine a company discovers that one of its products has a minor safety flaw unlikely to harm most users. Should it issue a costly recall or simply disclose the risk to customers? Which do you believe is the more ethical choice, and why?

posts.name: Grace
posts.text: For me the ethical choice is a recall, even when it's expensive. A company that knows about a flaw has already accepted a duty to the people who trusted its product, and 'unlikely to harm most users' still means that some will be harmed. Cutting costs at the expense of even a few customers' safety crosses a clear moral line.

posts.name: Noah
posts.text: I think full disclosure can be the more reasonable choice in genuinely minor cases. A massive recall wastes resources and can even introduce new risks, while honest, prominent warnings respect customers' ability to decide for themselves. The key condition is that the disclosure must be truly transparent, not buried in fine print.

model_essay: I'm with Grace that a recall is the sounder ethical default, though Noah's conditions deserve real weight. The heart of the matter is who bears the risk. The company knows exactly how the flaw behaves; the customer, even a well-warned one, does not, and 'unlikely to harm most users' quietly accepts that a few will be hurt. Noah is right that some recalls waste resources, but disclosure shifts the burden onto the very people who trusted the product, and warnings get overlooked no matter how prominent they are. If a firm truly believes the risk is trivial, then the recall should be cheap too. When it isn't cheap, that cost is usually a signal that the danger is more real than the company wants to admit.

Course: Business Ethics

Professor: Imagine a company discovers it may have suffered a data breach but isn't yet certain any customer information was actually stolen. Should it disclose the incident immediately, or wait until it confirms real harm? Which is the more ethical choice, and why?

Yara: I think immediate disclosure is the ethical choice. The people whose data might be exposed are the ones who can act, by changing passwords or watching their accounts, and only if they're told. Staying silent to protect the company's image gambles with risks that aren't the company's to take alone.
Cole: I'd wait until there's something confirmed to report. Announcing a breach that may not have happened can cause needless panic, and repeated false alarms make customers ignore real warnings later. Responsible disclosure means being accurate, not just fast.
similar.model_essay: I side with Yara, though Cole names a real cost. The decisive question is who can act on the information. If customer data may be exposed, only the customers can protect themselves, by changing passwords or monitoring accounts, and they can't act on a warning they never receive. Silence keeps the risk on the very people who didn't choose it, which favors the company over those it serves. Cole is right that crying wolf breeds fatigue, but the fix isn't delay; it is honest wording that distinguishes a confirmed theft from a possible exposure. A message that says we are investigating and here are sensible precautions respects customers without inducing panic. When the harm falls on others, I would rather tell them early than protect the firm's image.

Course: Business Ethics

Professor: Suppose an online retailer accidentally lists a product far below cost, and thousands of customers order it before the error is caught. Is the company ethically obliged to honor the mistaken price, or is it acceptable to cancel the orders?

Ines: I don't think the company is obliged to honor an obvious mistake. Many buyers clearly knew the price was an error and rushed to exploit it. Forcing a small business to sell at a devastating loss over an honest slip seems unfair, as long as it cancels politely and quickly.
Rafael: I lean the other way. The company set the price and controls its own systems, so the mistake is theirs to own. Customers made real plans around a confirmed order, and honoring it, or at least offering a fair discount, protects the trust a business depends on.
similar.model_essay: This one is genuinely close, but I land between Ines and Rafael, tilting toward Ines with a condition. Rafael is right that the company controls its own systems, so it can't simply shrug off responsibility, and a confirmed order does create a real expectation. Yet intent matters ethically. When a price is obviously, absurdly wrong, many buyers aren't relying in good faith; they are knowingly exploiting a slip, which weakens their moral claim. So I would not force a small firm into ruinous losses. What I would require is that it own the error gracefully: cancel promptly, apologize, and offer a genuine goodwill gesture such as a voucher. Course: Business Ethics

Professor: A company can lower costs by producing in a country where wages and working conditions are legal but far below its home standards. Is it ethical to continue operating there, or does the firm have a duty to demand better conditions?

Wanjiru: I believe the firm has a duty to do better than the local minimum. 'It's legal there' can't be the whole moral test, since laws in some places barely protect workers. A company profiting from those low costs shares responsibility for the conditions that make them possible.

professor: Many corporations now publicize their commitments to social and environmental causes. Do you think businesses have a genuine responsibility to address social problems, or should they focus strictly on profit and leave such issues to governments?

Course: Corporate Responsibility

similar.model_essay: This turns on fairness, and I lean toward Idris while taking Valentina's condition seriously. The elegant move is to earmark the pricing revenue for the transit she wants, so the fee funds the alternative rather than assuming it. professor: When a serious new health threat emerges, governments must choose between strict measures that limit personal freedom and voluntary guidelines that rely on public cooperation. Which approach do you think better protects a community's health?

model_essay: For a genuinely serious threat, I come down on Mei's side, though Andre is right about the long game. So the resolution is sequencing: act decisively while the threat is sharp, but justify every measure transparently and lift it the moment it's no longer warranted. Cyrus is right that voluntary uptake often plateaus below the threshold needed to halt spread, and that those who cannot be vaccinated, newborns, the immune-compromised, depend on everyone else. A requirement with narrow medical exemptions is a reasonable price for shielding them. Yet Wanjiru identifies a real danger: mandates imposed clumsily can entrench distrust that outlasts the emergency and undermines the next campaign. The two aren't wholly opposed. The strongest approach earns trust first, making vaccines free, convenient, and clearly explained, so only the resistant minority remains, then reserves requirements for the settings where the vulnerable are most exposed. Persuasion should carry most of the work; the law should cover the gap it can't quite close.

Course: Public Health

Professor: To reduce the harms of smoking, some governments ban it in all indoor public spaces, while others prefer designated smoking areas paired with education. Which approach do you think better protects public health, and why?

Hugo: I support comprehensive bans. Secondhand smoke harms everyone nearby, so this isn't only about the smoker's choice, it's about the waiter or bystander breathing it involuntarily. Bans on indoor smoking cut heart-attack rates measurably wherever they've been tried, and designated areas leak smoke and imply that some exposure is acceptable.
Dalia: I'd prefer designated areas and education. Outright bans can feel heavy-handed and simply push smokers into doorways, and they do little to change the habit itself. Helping people understand the risks and offering support to quit tackles the root cause, whereas a ban just relocates the smoke without addressing why people smoke in the first place.
similar.model_essay: I side firmly with Hugo, though Dalia's emphasis on quitting still matters. The decisive fact is the one Hugo stresses: secondhand smoke harms bystanders who never chose it, so this isn't purely a personal decision, and the waiter breathing it deserves protection. The evidence is clear, with indoor-smoking bans followed by measurable drops in heart-attack admissions, while designated areas leak and imply that some involuntary exposure is fine. Dalia is right that bans don't by themselves cure the addiction, and I'd pair them with the education and quitting support she wants. But those are complements, not substitutes. Protecting non-smokers can't wait for smokers to be persuaded, so the ban does the urgent work while education addresses the habit. Both, with the ban as the floor.

Course: Public Health

Professor: To keep restaurant food safe, authorities can enforce strict inspections and rules or instead rely on published hygiene ratings that let customers decide. Which approach do you think better protects public health, and why?

Farid: I think strict inspections are essential. Foodborne illness can hospitalize or kill, and by the time customers could react to a bad reputation, people have already been harmed. Ratings assume diners can judge a hidden kitchen, which they can't. Enforceable standards catch dangers before anyone gets sick, which is exactly what public health should do.
Esther: I'd lean on transparency and ratings. Posting clear hygiene grades pressures every restaurant to compete on cleanliness, and it respects customers' ability to choose. Heavy inspection regimes are costly, can be inconsistent, and often burden small family restaurants most, whereas visible ratings harness ordinary customers to keep standards high day to day.
similar.model_essay: I come down on Farid's side, while keeping Esther's transparency as a valuable add-on. Farid identifies why this problem resists a market solution: a diner cannot see the kitchen, and foodborne illness harms people before any reputation adjusts, so waiting for customers to punish a bad restaurant means someone is already sick. Enforceable standards act before harm, precisely public health's job. Esther is right that published grades create pressure and respect customer choice, and I'd keep them; the best systems post inspection results publicly. But ratings work because inspections generate them; transparency is the complement, not the foundation. Relying on ratings alone assumes diners can judge a hidden risk they can't observe. Mandatory inspection has to be the floor, with public grades amplifying it.

professor: Some public health campaigns focus on educating individuals to make healthier choices, while others push for structural changes such as taxes on sugary drinks. Which strategy do you think is more effective at improving a whole population's health? Why?

posts.name: Sara
posts.text: I think structural changes like taxes on sugary drinks tend to work better. Individual education is easily drowned out by advertising and by the plain fact that unhealthy options are cheap and everywhere. Reshaping the environment so the healthy choice becomes the easy choice reaches everyone, not only those who happen to be paying attention.

posts.name: Tom
posts.text: I'd put more emphasis on educating individuals first. Taxes and bans can feel paternalistic and often hit lower-income people hardest without changing the beliefs behind their habits. When people truly understand why a choice matters, the change tends to stick and to spread into the rest of their lives.

model_essay: I'm persuaded by Sara that structural changes usually do more for a whole population's health. Education is valuable, but it competes against billion-dollar advertising and against the plain fact that the cheapest, most available options are often the unhealthiest. Reshaping that environment, so the healthy choice is also the easy and affordable one, reaches everyone rather than only the attentive few. Tom's objections aren't empty: taxes can feel paternalistic and, if poorly designed, can hit lower-income families hardest. But that's an argument for careful design, not retreat, since the revenue can fund subsidies that flip the regressive effect. And education isn't the opposite of structural policy; it works best beside it. As the primary lever, though, changing the environment simply outperforms asking people to resist it by willpower alone.

The measures that actually moved smoking rates, sharp taxes, advertising bans, smoke-free public spaces, work on the environment rather than on willpower, and reach every smoker at once. If someone isn't motivated to move, even the best-designed city won't make them, so the mindset has to come first.

Paulo's insight is that behavior follows environment more reliably than motivation follows a lecture: when walking and cycling are the easy, safe way to reach daily destinations, activity accumulates without anyone scheduling it, whereas exhortation collides with a day built around sitting. That reach, everyone in the city at once, is what education can't match. Jana is right that rebuilding a city is slow and costly; I wouldn't drop education entirely. But her 'mindset first' order gets it backwards, since even motivated people give up when streets are hostile to walking. So I'd change the environment first and let education ride alongside, rather than ask willpower to overcome a city built against movement.

Course: Public Health Policy

Professor: To reduce the harms of heavy drinking, governments can run awareness campaigns aimed at individuals or impose structural limits such as minimum pricing and restrictions on where alcohol is sold and advertised. Which approach do you think is more effective, and why?

Zaid: I favor awareness campaigns. Drinking is bound up with culture and personal choice, and heavy-handed limits breed resentment and black markets rather than healthier habits. Helping people understand the risks and recognize their own patterns respects their autonomy and produces change they actually own, instead of rules that feel like the state policing private life.
Odile: I think structural limits work better. Research consistently shows alcohol harm tracks price and availability, so minimum pricing and fewer outlets cut the damage across a whole population, especially the heaviest drinkers. Campaigns are easily drowned out by pervasive advertising and cheap drinks, whereas changing price and access reaches everyone, not just the already health-conscious.
similar.model_essay: I find Odile's case stronger, while granting Zaid's concern about overreach. Zaid is right that drinking is cultural and that clumsy prohibition can backfire into resentment or black markets, so I wouldn't lurch toward bans. But the evidence Odile cites is hard to dismiss: alcohol harm tracks price and availability closely, and measures like minimum unit pricing reduce consumption most among the heaviest drinkers, exactly the group campaigns reach least. Awareness competes against saturating advertising and cheap, ubiquitous drink, an uneven fight that favors structure over slogans. Zaid's autonomy point is best honored not by dropping structural measures but by choosing proportionate ones, nudging price and access rather than forbidding, while keeping education alongside. Reshaping the environment does the population-level work persuasion alone has repeatedly failed to deliver.

professor: With so much news now flowing through social media, some believe citizens are better informed than ever, while others think the spread of misinformation has left the public less informed. On balance, has social media improved or harmed public understanding of important issues?

posts.name: Layla
posts.text: On balance I think social media has harmed public understanding. The same platforms that circulate solid reporting spread rumors just as quickly, and their algorithms reward outrage over accuracy. Many people now feel well informed while actually absorbing a stream of misleading headlines they never bother to verify.

posts.name: Felix
posts.text: I take a more positive view. Social media lets ordinary people reach reporting and firsthand accounts that traditional gatekeepers once filtered out, and it has surfaced stories that would otherwise have stayed hidden. Misinformation is a genuine danger, but the answer is stronger media literacy, not less access to information.

model_essay: On balance I side with Layla, while conceding that Felix names social media's real virtue. Felix is right that these platforms cracked open a gatekept media system and surfaced firsthand accounts the old outlets buried. The trouble is that the same architecture distributes falsehood just as efficiently as truth, and its engagement-driven algorithms actively favor whatever provokes the strongest reaction over whatever is accurate. The result is a public that often feels better informed while absorbing a stream of unverified headlines. Felix's remedy, stronger media literacy, is necessary but not sufficient, because it asks individuals to out-discipline systems engineered to capture their attention. More information is only a gain if the environment rewards accuracy, and right now it largely doesn't. That, for me, tips the balance toward harm.

Course: Media Studies

Professor: As local newspapers close and news moves online, some argue communities are better served by abundant free information, while others fear people are losing reliable coverage of the places they live. On balance, has this shift improved or harmed public understanding of local issues?

Ruth: On balance I think it's harmed local understanding. Online news is vast but mostly national or global, and when a town's newspaper dies, the patient coverage of its council, courts, and schools usually vanishes with it. People end up informed about distant controversies while knowing less and less about the decisions that shape their own streets.
Emil: I take a more hopeful view. Online tools let residents report and share local news directly, without waiting for a shrinking newsroom to notice, and community forums and newsletters now cover neighborhoods traditional papers ignored. The old local paper wasn't perfect either. The shift is painful, but it's opening new, more participatory ways of knowing our communities.
similar.model_essay: I share Ruth's concern more than Emil's optimism, though he identifies a real silver lining. Emil is right that online tools let residents cover neighborhoods old newsrooms overlooked, and that participatory reporting can fill some gaps. But Ruth names the harder loss: the patient, unglamorous coverage of councils, courts, and school boards that a local paper sustained rarely survives its closure, because volunteers seldom sit through budget meetings week after week. The result is a public flooded with national outrage yet blind to the decisions shaping its own streets, precisely where citizens have the most power to act. Emil's new forms are welcome, but so far they supplement rather than replace that watchdog role. Until they reliably do, the shift has, on balance, thinned local understanding rather than deepened it.

Course: Media Studies

Professor: Video platforms now serve as a major source of news and self-education for millions. Some celebrate this as democratized learning, while others worry it spreads confident misinformation. On balance, have such platforms improved or harmed the public's understanding of important topics?

Tobias: I think they've improved it overall. I've learned genuine skills and grasped complex topics from experts who could never have reached me through traditional media. For anyone curious, there's now free, vivid explanation of almost anything, which has opened knowledge that used to sit behind classrooms and paywalls.
Lena: I'm more worried. The same platforms serve up polished conspiracy theories with the same confident production as real expertise, and the algorithm can't tell them apart, it just promotes whatever holds attention. Many viewers come away feeling educated while having absorbed something misleading, which may be worse than knowing nothing at all.
similar.model_essay: This is close, but I tilt toward Tobias while taking Lena's warning to heart. Tobias is right that these platforms have democratized real expertise: skills and explanations once locked behind classrooms or paywalls are now free for anyone curious, and I've learned a great deal that way. Lena's danger is equally real, though, expert and charlatan arrive in the same slick format, and an algorithm optimizing for attention can't distinguish them. What tips me toward Tobias is that misinformation, while serious, is concentrated in certain contested topics, whereas the gains in ordinary learning are broad and quietly enormous. The honest position keeps his optimism conditional on Lena's remedy: the value is real, but only for viewers who check sources rather than trust confident delivery.

Course: Media Studies

Professor: Much online discussion now happens anonymously, in comment sections and forums where people speak without revealing who they are. Do you think this anonymity has enriched public debate by freeing people to speak, or degraded it? Explain your view.

Ravi: I think anonymity has degraded debate more than it's freed it. When names and consequences disappear, so does much of the civility that keeps a discussion productive, and threads fill with insults and bad faith. The loudest, cruelest voices dominate, which drives thoughtful people away and leaves the conversation coarser than it needs to be.
Nour: I'd defend anonymity's value. It lets people raise unpopular truths, whistleblowers, dissidents, or anyone fearing retaliation, that they'd never voice under their real names. Yes, it enables some ugliness, but forcing real identities would silence exactly the vulnerable voices that open debate most needs. The problem is moderation, not anonymity itself.
similar.model_essay: Both name something real, but on balance I lean toward Ravi for everyday discussion while conceding Nour's exception. Ravi is right that stripping away names and consequences erodes civility, letting the cruelest voices dominate and driving thoughtful people out, which coarsens debate rather than enriching it. Yet Nour identifies the case anonymity exists to protect: whistleblowers, dissidents, and the vulnerable can raise truths they'd never speak under their names, and forcing identity would silence them first. These describe different arenas. In ordinary comment sections, accountability improves the conversation; under real danger, anonymity is indispensable. So the answer isn't to abolish or absolutize it but to moderate well, protecting the exposed speaker while curbing the anonymous mob. Blanket anonymity, though, degrades more than it frees.

professor: Streaming services rely on algorithms to recommend content based on what we already watch and listen to. Do you think these personalized recommendations enrich our cultural experience, or do they narrow our tastes and limit what we discover?

posts.name: Nina
posts.text: I worry that recommendation algorithms mostly narrow our tastes. By constantly feeding us more of what we've already liked, they build a comfortable bubble that rarely challenges us with anything unfamiliar. I've noticed I discover far more surprising music from friends than from any 'recommended for you' list.

posts.name: Raj
posts.text: I think the algorithms enrich my experience more than they limit it. With such an overwhelming amount of content available, good recommendations help me find hidden works I'd never have stumbled on alone, including films from other countries. The tools reflect our own choices, so if we deliberately explore, they'll happily lead us somewhere new.

model_essay: I lean toward Nina, though Raj identifies the escape hatch. In principle Raj is right that an algorithm can surface a hidden film I'd never have found alone. But its objective isn't to broaden me; it's to keep me watching, and the safest way to do that is to serve more of what I already like. Left to run, that logic tightens the circle of my taste, exactly as Nina describes. Raj's condition, that we deliberately explore, is the crucial qualifier, but it also concedes the point: the system's default pulls toward comfort, and resisting it takes effort most viewers won't spend. My best discoveries, like Nina's, still come from friends handing me something I'd never have picked. The tools can enrich us, but only against their own grain.
